narrative:
Ashley Hinson represents Iowa's 2nd Congressional District, a sprawling stretch of eastern Iowa that includes Cedar Rapids, Iowa City's suburbs, and a patchwork of small towns and farmland. A former television news anchor at KCRG-TV in Cedar Rapids, Hinson flipped the seat from Democratic hands in 2020 after a single term in the Iowa state legislature, running on a telegenic smile and a message of fiscal conservatism wrapped in Midwestern politeness [1]. She sits on the Appropriations Committee, one of the most powerful assignments in the House, and has positioned herself as a rising star in the Republican conference, earning endorsements from leadership and a spot on the party's recruitment shortlist for higher office [2]. She was reelected in 2022 in a newly drawn district that leaned more Republican than her original seat, a redistricting outcome she did not publicly complain about.

On the 80% Bill's twenty planks, Hinson has staked out a handful of populist positions that play well in an agricultural district. She cosponsored the Protecting America's Agricultural Land from Foreign Harm Act, seeking to ban adversarial foreign governments from purchasing U.S. farmland — a natural crowd-pleaser in a state where corn and soybean acreage is practically a religion [3]. She has voiced support for congressional term limits, cosponsoring a term-limits constitutional amendment in the House [4]. She backed the push to release the Epstein client list, a zero-cost position with universal appeal [5]. And she supported the right-to-repair movement as it pertains to agricultural equipment, a genuinely consequential stance in a state where John Deere's repair monopoly is a kitchen-table issue [6].

But on the reforms that would require crossing her party's donor class, Hinson's populism evaporates. She voted against the Inflation Reduction Act and its Medicare drug price negotiation provisions, despite the fact that Iowa's seniors pay some of the highest prescription drug costs in the country [7]. She has not cosponsored legislation to ban congressional stock trading, has shown no interest in a lifetime lobbying ban, and has opposed the DISCLOSE Act and efforts to overturn Citizens United [8]. On auditing the Pentagon, banning dark money, ending gerrymandering, taxing the ultra-wealthy, closing the carried interest loophole, and establishing paid family leave, Hinson's record ranges from silent to actively opposed. She voted against repealing the 2002 Iraq AUMF when it came to the House floor, siding with the faction that believes perpetual war authorizations are a feature rather than a bug [9]. She has also shown no appetite for banning corporate PACs or corporate purchases of single-family homes, despite Iowa's growing housing affordability crisis.

The fundraising picture completes the portrait. In her 2024 cycle, Hinson raised approximately $5 million, a formidable war chest for an Iowa House seat, with significant contributions from agribusiness PACs, insurance industry PACs, real estate interests, and leadership PACs from Republican colleagues [10]. The American Farm Bureau Federation, the National Association of Realtors, and health insurance companies have been reliable backers, industries with direct stakes in the farm policy, housing policy, and health care legislation that flows through her committee work [11]. Corporate PAC money has constituted a meaningful share of her fundraising, which explains her silence on banning it. She has also benefited from dark money spending by outside groups aligned with Republican leadership, a dynamic she has shown no desire to reform [12].

Hinson is a polished communicator who knows how to signal populism without practicing it. She will fight for Iowa farmers' right to repair their own tractors and keep foreign adversaries from buying their land — fights that are real but also safe. On the structural reforms that would disrupt the flow of corporate money into congressional campaigns, force transparency on dark money, or give the government leverage to negotiate drug prices, she is as quiet as the anchor desk after the cameras stop rolling. In a district where voters overwhelmingly support these reforms, that silence is not modesty — it is strategy.

Erin Houchin was elected to represent Indiana's 9th Congressional District in 2022, filling the seat vacated by the retirement of Trey Hollingsworth, who apparently decided that one of the safest Republican seats in the country was not interesting enough to keep [1]. Houchin, a former Indiana state senator from Salem, had lost the 2022 Republican primary's precursor race in 2016 but came back to win handily the second time around. Her district covers the rural and suburban swath of southeastern Indiana from Bloomington's exurbs down to the Kentucky border — a deep-red stretch of the state where the general election is a formality and the real contest is proving who can out-conservative whom in the primary [2]. In Washington, she landed seats on the Financial Services Committee and the Education and Workforce Committee, assignments that put her at the intersection of banking regulation and labor policy — two areas where the 80% Bill has a great deal to say.

On the twenty issues Americans overwhelmingly agree on, Houchin's record is a masterclass in knowing which populist notes to hit and which to pretend do not exist. She has expressed support for congressional term limits, which is the free square on the Republican populism bingo card [3]. She has backed legislation to protect U.S. farmland from foreign purchases, a position that resonates in a district full of soybean and corn fields [4]. She has voiced support for releasing the Epstein files, another no-cost crowd-pleaser [5]. But that is roughly where the populism ends and the party-line voting begins. Houchin voted against the Inflation Reduction Act and its Medicare drug negotiation provisions, opposed the DISCLOSE Act, and has shown no interest in banning congressional stock trading, ending dark money, auditing the Pentagon, or taxing the ultra-wealthy [6]. On paid family leave, she has preferred to talk about "family values" without sponsoring legislation that would actually let parents stay home with a newborn. On gerrymandering, she is a beneficiary of Indiana's Republican-drawn maps and has displayed precisely zero interest in reforming the process that handed her a safe seat.

The Financial Services Committee seat is where things get particularly instructive. Houchin has opposed efforts to crack down on junk fees charged by banks and credit card companies, voting in line with the financial industry rather than the consumers in her district who pay those fees [7]. She has not backed banning corporate purchases of single-family homes, despite representing communities where corporate landlords have been steadily buying up housing stock [8]. The carried interest loophole, which allows wealthy fund managers to pay lower tax rates than Indiana teachers and nurses, has not drawn a whisper of concern from her office.

Her donor profile tells the familiar story. In her 2024 cycle, Houchin's top industry contributors included the securities and investment sector, real estate, and insurance companies — the very industries that benefit most from the loopholes and deregulation she protects from her perch on Financial Services [9]. PAC money from the American Bankers Association, the National Association of Realtors, and various corporate leadership PACs has flowed steadily into her campaign [10]. For a freshman congresswoman, she adapted to the Washington fundraising circuit with remarkable speed, which is either a testament to her political skill or a sign that the system is working exactly as designed — depending on your level of cynicism.

Erin Houchin is, in the end, a perfectly calibrated product of the modern Republican primary system: conservative enough to survive the primary, compliant enough to keep the donor class happy, and populist enough on the margins to run ads about term limits and farmland while quietly voting against every reform that would actually cost her contributors a dime. The voters of IN-9 deserve to know which of those loyalties comes first [11].

Val Hoyle arrived in Congress in 2023 as a third-generation union member and former Oregon Labor Commissioner, carrying the kind of progressive bona fides that make a campaign website practically write itself. She sits on the Transportation and Infrastructure Committee and the Natural Resources Committee — sensible assignments for a district with eleven ports and 85% forestland. Her voting record is reliably Democratic, she champions paid sick leave, and she talks a big game about holding corporations accountable. So far, so good. Then you look at the stock portfolio.

In September 2025, OpenSecrets revealed that Hoyle had violated the STOCK Act by failing to disclose 217 individual stock transactions made by her husband Stephen — trades valued between $245,000 and $3.35 million. Among the late-reported holdings? Defense contractors Lockheed Martin and Northrop Grumman. The penalty for this spectacular lapse in transparency was $200 — roughly the price of a nice dinner in Portland, paid personally by Hoyle so taxpayers wouldn't have to shoulder the crushing burden. Her office explained that the trades were made by her husband's financial broker without specific direction, arising from a retirement plan switch from a pension to an individually managed 401(k). The classic "my financial adviser did it" defense, beloved by members of Congress since time immemorial.

Here is where the comedy becomes truly artful: Hoyle is a cosponsor of the TRUST in Congress Act, which would ban congressional stock trading, and the Stop Politicians Profiting from War Act of 2025, which would specifically prohibit lawmakers and their families from owning defense industry stocks. She is, in other words, sponsoring legislation to ban the exact behavior her household was engaged in while she wasn't paying attention. After the violation came to light, Hoyle announced that her husband's retirement account was being transferred into mutual funds to "avoid any potential conflict of interest" — a conflict that apparently only became apparent once reporters started asking questions.

To her credit, Hoyle did eventually support the consolidated bipartisan Restore Trust in Congress Act and has pushed for a House floor vote. She championed the Inflation Reduction Act's Medicare drug price negotiation provisions, telling constituents that the $35 insulin cap showed "Democrats in Congress actually want to do something." As Oregon's former labor commissioner, she expanded apprenticeship programs and fought to extend the Oregon Family Leave Act during the pandemic — real accomplishments with real impact on working families.

But 217 undisclosed stock trades is not a rounding error. It is a systemic failure of the disclosure regime that Hoyle herself acknowledges is broken. The question is whether she is the firefighter or the arsonist — and the honest answer is that in Congress, those roles are not mutually exclusive. She will vote to ban stock trading. She will also benefit from the system until that ban passes. And the $200 fine ensures that the cost of getting caught remains, for members of Congress, roughly equivalent to a parking ticket.

Wesley Hunt, the self-proclaimed "Energy Congressman of the World," has represented Texas's 38th District since 2023 — though "represented" is doing some heavy lifting given that he missed 78 percent of House votes between January 2025 and March 2026. The reason? He was too busy running for Senate to bother showing up for the job voters already gave him. When confronted about his absenteeism, Hunt pointed to JD Vance's missed votes during his presidential campaign, which is the congressional equivalent of telling your boss that your coworker also takes two-hour lunches.

Hunt's brief tenure has been defined less by legislation than by loyalty. He chairs the Small Business Committee's Rural Development, Energy, and Supply Chains subcommittee and serves on Judiciary and Natural Resources — committee seats he has used primarily to champion more oil and gas drilling while declaring that America needs "energy addition, not energy transition." His top donors reflect the message: Enterprise Products Partners poured $131,900 into his victory fund in 2020, and the oil and gas sector has remained a reliable ATM throughout his career. On banning corporate PACs or closing dark money loopholes, Hunt has been as silent as a wellhead at midnight.

Then there is the Elon Musk situation. A ProPublica investigation revealed that Hunt spent months quietly helping Musk's Boring Company pitch a $760 million plan to build tunnels under Houston, with the company requesting 15 percent of the cost upfront from state and local coffers. Nothing says small government quite like funneling three-quarters of a billion in public money to the world's richest man's side project.

The House Ethics Committee also investigated Hunt for spending over $74,000 in campaign funds at the Post Oak Hotel's exclusive Oak Room club between 2022 and 2024. He was ultimately cleared — the committee found no evidence of "intentional" misuse — but noted his campaign did not fully comply with finance standards. The committee then updated its guidance, which is Washington's way of saying "we changed the rules so this stops being embarrassing."

On the 80% Bill checklist, Hunt's record is a near-total blank. No movement on stock trading bans, no support for Pentagon audits despite serving alongside the Armed Services Committee's jurisdiction, no interest in term limits, paid family leave, or banning junk fees. He voted to codify DOGE's findings on wasteful spending, which sounds reform-minded until you realize it means endorsing Elon Musk's cost-cutting recommendations — the same Musk whose tunneling company Hunt was simultaneously helping secure government contracts. The conflict of interest is so circular it could power a wind turbine, if Hunt believed in those.

Harriet Hageman rode into Congress in 2022 on the strength of one qualification: Donald Trump told Wyoming to replace Liz Cheney, and Hageman raised her hand. A natural resources attorney who spent her career representing oil, gas, and ranching interests against federal regulators, Hageman brought exactly one skill set to Washington: the ability to fight the government on behalf of the industries that paid her bills. She now sits on the House Natural Resources and Judiciary Committees, which is a bit like hiring a fox to audit the henhouse and then giving it a second henhouse for good measure.

On the Judiciary Committee, Hageman has made a name for herself by calling the FBI and DOJ "corrupt," championing January 6 defendants, and questioning whether the Capitol attack even qualifies as an insurrection. She warned Attorney General Garland not to block the release of a January 6 report, then used her platform to try to rewrite the narrative of that day entirely. Her Heritage Action score of 98 percent confirms what everyone already knew: she votes the hard-right party line with the mechanical consistency of a metronome.

Her March 2025 town hall in Laramie became a national story when over 500 constituents packed the auditorium to confront her about DOGE cuts, federal land sales, and the gutting of national parks. When asked about the future of Grand Teton and Yellowstone, Hageman told the crowd, "It's so bizarre to me how obsessed you are with the federal government." The crowd booed. She called them "hysterical" and cut the event 15 minutes short. Days later, she announced she was switching all future town halls to virtual-only, citing "Democrat threats of violence" — a creative way to avoid being booed by your own constituents.

On public lands, Hageman backed Senator Mike Lee's provision to mandate the sale of BLM and Forest Service lands in Western states, describing the potential sell-off of up to 250 million acres as "narrow and community-focused." The Wilderness Society, the Outdoor Recreation Roundtable, and over 500 Wyomingites who rallied at the state capitol disagreed. In December 2025, Hageman announced she would run for Senate in 2026 to replace the retiring Cynthia Lummis, ensuring that Wyoming's tradition of sending fossil fuel attorneys to Washington remains unbroken.

On the 80% Bill's twenty planks, Hageman opposes essentially all of them. Ban corporate PACs? Hers are funded by the oil and gas industry. Protect farmland and ban corporate home buying? She represents the industries doing the buying. Paid family leave, Medicare drug negotiation, the DISCLOSE Act? Not a chance. Her voting record is a monument to corporate loyalty dressed up as frontier independence.

Abraham "Abe" Hamadeh is the representative for Arizona's 8th Congressional District, a former Army Reserve intelligence officer, ex-Maricopa County prosecutor, and the first Arab American elected to Congress from Arizona [1]. He is also, notably, a man who lost the 2022 race for Arizona attorney general by 280 votes and then filed seven successive lawsuits to overturn the result—every one of which he lost—before the Arizona Supreme Court sanctioned him for misrepresenting facts to the court [2][3]. Undeterred by this judicial rebuke, he ran for Congress in 2024, won, and now sits on the Armed Services and Veterans' Affairs committees, where he focuses on border security, defense spending, and legislation for veterans [4].

On the 80% Bill's twenty items, Hamadeh occupies an uncomfortable middle ground between populist rhetoric and party-line voting. He signed the U.S. Term Limits pledge during his campaign, promising to cosponsor a resolution capping House members at three terms—then broke that pledge once in office, earning a billboard in his district from U.S. Term Limits calling him out for "playing the D.C. game" [5]. He voted for the One Big Beautiful Bill, the sprawling Republican reconciliation package that the Tax Policy Center described as "designed for the wealthy," which notably failed to close the carried interest loophole despite Trump himself calling for it [6][7]. That vote puts him squarely against Bills 4 (Tax the Ultra-Wealthy), 17 (Close Tax Loopholes), and arguably 11 (Fund Social Security), since the bill adds trillions to the deficit. On fair elections (Bill 8), his record of filing sanctioned election lawsuits speaks louder than any campaign website.

Where Hamadeh theoretically aligns with popular will is on items like releasing the Epstein files (Bill 15), which passed the House with broad bipartisan support, and banning congressional stock trading (Bill 1), which Trump endorsed in his State of the Union—though the version Trump backed was riddled with loopholes that allow lawmakers to shift profits into bonds, crypto, and mutual funds [8]. Hamadeh has not been a vocal champion of Pentagon audits (Bill 6) despite sitting on Armed Services, a committee with direct oversight of a department that has failed its audit eight consecutive times [9]. On the AUMF repeal (Bill 2) and the lifetime lobbying ban (Bill 3), he has been silent, which in Washington is its own kind of answer.

Follow the money and the picture sharpens. Hamadeh's 2024 campaign was supported by the Republican ecosystem that includes the Oil & Gas Workers Association endorsement and significant support from the RAGA network, which spent over $797,000 backing his 2022 attorney general bid [10][11]. His OpenSecrets profile shows industry contributions flowing from the usual Republican donor base, though as a freshman his corporate PAC footprint is still developing [12]. The question is whether the man who campaigned as a populist outsider—"a fighter for the American people"—will vote like one when it means crossing the donors who got him there.

The bottom line on Hamadeh is that he talks the talk on a few 80% Bill items when Trump makes them fashionable, but votes the party line when it counts. He broke his term limits pledge within months. He voted for a bill that showers tax breaks on the wealthy while ignoring the carried interest loophole. And his commitment to election integrity is, to put it charitably, selective—fiercely dedicated when he loses, conspicuously quiet when the system works in his favor. Arizona's 8th deserves to know the difference between campaign promises and congressional votes.

Mike Haridopolos is a Republican representing Florida's 8th Congressional District, which covers the Space Coast — Brevard County, home to Kennedy Space Center and Patrick Space Force Base. Before arriving in Washington, Haridopolos served as President of the Florida State Senate from 2010 to 2012, a tenure memorable less for legislative achievement than for a controversy involving a $152,000 book contract with Brevard Community College to write a tome on the history of the Florida Republican Party, a book that was never meaningfully completed [1]. He briefly ran for U.S. Senate in 2012 before dropping out, then spent a decade in the private sector before winning his House seat in 2024 [2]. His return to public life suggests either a renewed sense of civic duty or a calculation that the political climate had sufficiently forgotten the book deal.

On the 80% Bill's twenty provisions, Haridopolos's positions track closely with the Republican leadership playbook: enthusiastic about the items that cost his donor base nothing and conspicuously absent on those that would. He has expressed support for congressional term limits [3], a perennial favorite among politicians who arrived in Washington recently enough to believe the sentiment sounds fresh. He has also backed measures to protect U.S. farmland from foreign acquisition [4]. But on banning congressional stock trading, auditing the Pentagon, taxing the ultra-wealthy, or closing the carried interest loophole, Haridopolos has offered no legislation and no public commitment. Given that his district's economy is deeply intertwined with defense contracting, his silence on a Pentagon audit is less an oversight than a survival strategy.

On campaign finance reform, Haridopolos is similarly quiet. He has not co-sponsored the DISCLOSE Act, has taken no position on banning corporate PACs or dark money, and has shown no interest in overturning Citizens United [5]. He has not championed Medicare drug price negotiation, paid family leave, police body cameras, or the right to repair. On ending forever wars through AUMF repeal, a position supported by large bipartisan majorities of voters, he has aligned with the hawkish consensus that prefers to leave those authorizations conveniently in place [6]. His record on banning corporate purchases of single-family homes and junk fees is nonexistent.

Follow the money and the pattern crystallizes. Haridopolos's donor base is heavy with defense and aerospace interests, reflecting his district's economic DNA [7]. PACs affiliated with Lockheed Martin, L3Harris Technologies, and other defense contractors with major Space Coast operations have been prominent supporters [7]. Real estate interests and Republican leadership PACs round out the picture [8]. When your top donors build rockets for the Pentagon, proposing an audit of their biggest customer is not a conversation you are eager to start.

Haridopolos is, in essence, a Space Coast conservative whose populist instincts extend precisely as far as his donor list permits. He will champion term limits and farmland protection — reforms that threaten no one writing him checks — while maintaining a disciplined silence on the structural changes that majorities of Americans actually want. The 80% Bill is a mirror, and Haridopolos would prefer not to look.

Jeff Hurd is the Republican congressman representing Colorado's sprawling 3rd District, a vast stretch of Western Slope ranches, ski towns, and energy country that previously belonged to Lauren Boebert before she decamped for greener pastures in the 4th. A Grand Junction attorney with degrees from Notre Dame, the University of Denver, and Columbia Law School, Hurd clerked for a federal appeals court judge and practiced at Sullivan & Cromwell in New York before returning to Western Colorado to represent rural electric cooperatives [1]. He won the 2024 GOP primary with about 41% of the vote, then beat Democrat Adam Frisch 50.8% to 45.8% in November, positioning himself as the serious, credentialed alternative to Boebert's chaos [2]. He sits on the Natural Resources, Transportation and Infrastructure, and Science committees [3].

On the 80% Bill's twenty priorities, Hurd is largely a non-participant. He has taken no public position on banning congressional stock trading, ending dark money, imposing a lifetime lobbying ban, taxing the ultra-wealthy, banning corporate PACs, auditing the Pentagon, funding Social Security, or congressional term limits. He holds approximately $75,800 in publicly traded assets but has not joined any of the bipartisan stock trading ban efforts circulating in the House [4]. On the Epstein files, the DISCLOSE Act, paid family leave, right to repair, and junk fees, his record is similarly blank. His campaign website focuses on energy, water, immigration, and public lands -- the issues that matter to his donors, not necessarily the 80% of Americans demanding structural reform [5].

Where Hurd does intersect with popular opinion, he manages to land on the wrong side. He was one of twelve House Republicans who signed a letter in April 2025 vowing they "cannot and will not support a final reconciliation bill that includes any reduction in Medicaid coverage for vulnerable populations" [6]. Three months later, he voted yes on Trump's Big Beautiful Bill, which the Congressional Budget Office estimates will cut $1 trillion from Medicaid over the next decade and add over $3 trillion to the national debt [7][8]. His explanation -- "It's not perfect" but "delivers on so many of the key priorities that we campaigned on" -- is the kind of pivot that makes voters wonder what those letters to leadership were actually for [9]. He was one of six Republicans who voted to rescind Trump's tariffs on Canada, suggesting an occasional independent streak, but it has not extended to any of the anti-corruption or economic populist measures in the 80% Bill [10].

The money trail explains the agenda. Hurd left the oil and gas industry to run for Congress, and the industry returned the favor handsomely. The Colorado Times Recorder reported that Big Oil "powered his election campaign," with major contributions from the firm he had been working for and its industry allies [11]. Once in office, Hurd introduced the Productive Public Lands Act in March 2025, which would undo Biden-era Bureau of Land Management protections on 2.3 million acres in Colorado alone, rolling back habitat protections for the Gunnison sage grouse and big game species to make way for drilling and mining [12][13]. As Alainna Belknap of Colorado Common Cause put it: "We have to follow the money" [11]. His official priorities page reads like a wish list drafted by the American Petroleum Institute: "energy dominance," reopening federal lands, and fighting what he calls regulatory overreach [5].

Jeff Hurd is, in fairness, exactly what he advertised: a polished, industry-friendly Republican who will fight for oil and gas while offering occasional moderate gestures that evaporate when the vote counts. But on the twenty reforms that unite 80% of Americans across party lines -- cleaning up Congress, reining in corporate influence, making the wealthy pay their share -- he is functionally absent. The 3rd District traded Boebert's spectacle for Hurd's competence, but the policy output, measured against what voters actually want, remains remarkably similar.

Pat Harrigan wants you to know he is a Green Beret. A West Point graduate in nuclear engineering, a combat veteran of Afghanistan, a man who managed 350 personnel and $100 million in infrastructure at age 23 [1]. He would also like you to know he owns a firearms empire -- ZRODelta, UnBrandedAR, US Optics -- sprawled across a 120,000-square-foot, 76-acre manufacturing complex in North Carolina, the only facility in America where guns, optics, and ammunition are all made under one roof [2]. What he is less eager to discuss is what happens when the man who manufactures AR-15s for a living gets to vote on gun legislation.

Harrigan co-sponsored the Concealed Carry Reciprocity Act, declaring that "the right to self-defense doesn't stop at a state line" [3]. He sits on the House Armed Services Committee, overseeing the very defense budget that funds contracts for companies like his own [4]. The phrase "conflict of interest" does not appear on his congressional website.

In his first term, Harrigan voted for the Laken Riley Act (264-159), the SAVE Act to require proof of citizenship for voter registration, and -- in a party-line squeaker at 218-214 -- the One Big Beautiful Bill Act, which slashed $1 trillion from health programs and $120 billion from SNAP [5][6][7]. He introduced the Families First Housing Act and a bill to promote Taiwan's energy security, though neither has advanced beyond referral [8]. His legislative focus areas -- Armed Forces (40%), Crime and Law Enforcement (40%) -- read like a product catalog for his day job [9].

On the 80% Bill's platform, Harrigan's record is a wall of silence and opposition. He has taken no position on banning congressional stock trading, ending dark money, or closing tax loopholes. He opposes Medicare drug negotiation expansion and has shown zero interest in Pentagon audits -- perhaps because the defense gravy train runs directly through his 76 acres in Burke County [10]. His campaign raised heavily from the defense and firearms sectors in 2024, though the exact figures are shielded behind PAC structures that would be illegal under the very transparency reforms he opposes [11].

Harrigan missed 7 of 449 roll call votes (1.6%), which is respectable -- he shows up to vote against your interests with admirable consistency [12]. He is proof that you can serve your country heroically in Afghanistan and then come home to serve the firearms industry from the floor of Congress.

Mark Harris is a man who believes in second chances -- at least for himself. The former senior pastor of First Baptist Church in Charlotte and president of the North Carolina Baptist Convention, Harris first tried to reach Congress in 2018. He appeared to win North Carolina's 9th District by 905 votes, until it emerged that his campaign operative, McCrae Dowless, had orchestrated an illegal absentee ballot harvesting scheme in Bladen County [1]. Dowless's workers collected blank and incomplete ballots from voters' doorsteps, forged signatures, and filled in votes [2]. Harris received 437 of 456 absentee votes in Bladen County -- a statistical miracle that required divine intervention, or a felony. The state Board of Elections voted 9-0 not to certify the results and ordered a new election [3]. Harris, citing ill health, declined to run again. Dowless was indicted and died awaiting trial [4].

None of this prevented Harris from running again in 2024 in the newly drawn 8th District, winning, and taking office on January 3, 2025. He was never criminally charged. In Washington, he joined the Freedom Caucus and has maintained a perfect voting attendance record -- 0 of 449 roll call votes missed [5][6]. His legislative priorities center on Crime and Law Enforcement (45%), Education (18%), and Immigration (18%) [7].

Before entering politics, Harris led the campaign for North Carolina's Amendment 1 in 2012, which banned same-sex marriage in the state constitution. He was a founding member of Vote for Marriage NC and mobilized churches across the state to support the measure [8]. The amendment was struck down as unconstitutional in 2014, and the Supreme Court's Obergefell decision in 2015 rendered it moot [9]. Harris has called for abolishing the Department of Education and has described being gay as a choice [10].

In Congress, Harris voted to advance the One Big Beautiful Bill Act, celebrating it as unlocking "the America First agenda" [11]. He has introduced bills related to child protection, homeschool recognition, and restricting abortion-related travel -- a legislative portfolio that reads like a Baptist Convention resolution with a congressional seal [12]. He has publicly committed to the Hyde Amendment's protections against taxpayer-funded abortion, telling the Daily Signal, "I don't envision anything coming to the floor that would in any way impede the protection of life" [13].

On the 80% Bill's platform, Harris offers nothing. No support for stock trading bans, dark money disclosure, Medicare drug negotiation, or paid family leave. He is a pastor who preached about honesty, hired a ballot fraudster, lost his election, waited five years, and came back to vote against everything 80% of Americans want.
